coredata.dc:title: Engineering phosphorous doped graphene quantum dots decorated on graphene hydrogel as effective photocatalyst and high-current density electrocatalyst for seawater splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Van Tam, Tran
Chandra Bhamu, Kailash
Jae Kim, Min
Gu Kang, Sung
Suk Chung, Jin
Hyun Hur, Seung
Mook Choi, Won
coredata.dc:description: Designing metal-free catalyst with outstanding activity for both photocatalytic and electrocatalytic seawater splitting is highly demand and still remained challenge. This work demonstrates the synthesis of P-doped graphene quantum dots (PGQDs) containing different P-configuration bonding of PC3, PO4 and PO3 via synthesis temperature controlled simple hydrothermal process. Specifically, PGQDs having PC3-bonds (PGQD3) shows effectively tunable electronic structure, significant enhancing visible light absorption, then their hybrid composite with graphene hydrogel (PGQD3/GH) is prepared. The PGQD3/GH composite possesses excellent photocatalytic hydrogen production with yield of 20.47 mmol·g−1·h−1 in seawater splitting. Furthermore, the PGQD3/GH composite performs superior electrochemical activities of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) toward seawater with an overpontetial of 41 mV at 10 mA·cm−2 as well as oxygen evolution reaction (OER) with 262 mV at 10 mA·cm−2. In seawater electrolysis cell, the PGQD3/GH composite delivers small overall voltage of 1.62 V to obtain high current density of 500 mA·cm−2 with excellent stability for 1000 h continuous operation, outperforming the commercial Pt/C and IrO2. This work demonstrates the role of PC3-bond in modulating the band gap and active site for photocatalytic and electrocatalytic seawater splitting reaction that may provide new idea for developing porous non-metal all-carbon catalyst for seawater splitting by both photocatalysis and electrocatalysis.

head.ce:title: Engineering phosphorous doped graphene quantum dots decorated on graphene hydrogel as effective photocatalyst and high-current density electrocatalyst for seawater splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro Nam-gu, Ulsan 680-749, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro Nam-gu, Ulsan 680-749, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing metal-free catalyst with outstanding activity for both photocatalytic and electrocatalytic seawater splitting is highly demand and still remained challenge. This work demonstrates the synthesis of P-doped graphene quantum dots (PGQDs) containing different P-configuration bonding of PC3, PO4 and PO3 via synthesis temperature controlled simple hydrothermal process. Specifically, PGQDs having PC3-bonds (PGQD3) shows effectively tunable electronic structure, significant enhancing visible light absorption, then their hybrid composite with graphene hydrogel (PGQD3/GH) is prepared. The PGQD3/GH composite possesses excellent photocatalytic hydrogen production with yield of 20.47 mmol·g−1·h−1 in seawater splitting. Furthermore, the PGQD3/GH composite performs superior electrochemical activities of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) toward seawater with an overpontetial of 41 mV at 10 mA·cm−2 as well as oxygen evolution reaction (OER) with 262 mV at 10 mA·cm−2. In seawater electrolysis cell, the PGQD3/GH composite delivers small overall voltage of 1.62 V to obtain high current density of 500 mA·cm−2 with excellent stability for 1000 h continuous operation, outperforming the commercial Pt/C and IrO2. This work demonstrates the role of PC3-bond in modulating the band gap and active site for photocatalytic and electrocatalytic seawater splitting reaction that may provide new idea for developing porous non-metal all-carbon catalyst for seawater splitting by both photocatalysis and electrocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) has emerged as a pivotal component in the pursuit of climate-safe and net-zero energy development, which has become an ambitious objective for numerous countries [1–3]. To achieve a decarbonized economy and reduce dependence on fossil fuels, significant efforts have been dedicated to the production of green H2 from renewable energy resources [4–8]. Consequently, visible-light photocatalysts [9,10] and water electrolysis [11,12] have garnered considerable attention as promising strategies for generating green H2. However, large-scale H2 production necessitates the utilization of catalysts that are low-cost, mass-producible, and highly efficient. While noble metals and their oxides, such as Pt, Ir, and Ru, have exhibited excellent catalytic activities for electrochemical and photocatalytic water splitting [13–15], their high cost and limited availability hinder their practical applications. Therefore, it is still challenging to develop the earth-abundant, cost-effective, and highly active catalysts as substitutes for noble metals. Among various materials developed [16], graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received significant attention as a promising metal-free catalyst for both electrocatalytic and photocatalytic water splitting. This is due to their unique properties, such as non-toxicity, ease of preparation, tunable photoluminescence, favorable physicochemical behavior, and surface modularity [17,18]. To control the band gap and enhance catalytic activity, researchers have explored the doping of GQDs with heteroatoms such as S, N, Cl, O, and P [19,20]. The introduction of heteroatoms affects the electroneutrality of graphene by promoting electron delocalization, leading to changes in charge distribution and electron configuration. Particularly, phosphorus (P) is interesting as it possesses valence electrons in the third shell and a lower electronegativity compared to carbon atoms. This results in the donation of electrons from P atoms into the hexagonal carbon framework, leading to a significant polarization in the phosphorus-carbon (P-C) bond. Furthermore, the orbital coupling effect between 2p of carbon atoms and 3p of P atoms in graphite-like P (PC3) structure can promote electron density near Fermi level, which may result in smaller band gap, enhanced electron transfer and improved electrochemical activity. These characteristics make phosphorus a suitable choice for modulating both the optical properties and electrochemical performance of graphene quantum dots. However, only few researches have been conducted on application of P doped GQD. Nobly, previous approaches for synthesizing P-doped graphene quantum dots (PGQDs) have exhibited low doping levels of PC3 and involved complicated procedures with high temperatures and time-consuming processes.

The demand for freshwater has increased significantly due to extensive consumption and limited resources, resulting from rapid population growth. Consequently, seawater electrolysis has emerged as a promising technique for hydrogen (H2) generation as an alternative to freshwater electrolysis. Seawater, being an abundant resource, covers 97.5 % of the earth which can be supplied unlimitedly. However, the practical application of seawater electrolysis for H2 production faces several challenges. One of the major obstacles is the competition between the chloride evolution reaction (ClER) and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode, primarily due to the presence of chloride ions (Cl-) in seawater. This competition leads to the formation of hypochlorite (ClO-) at high voltage operations, which is a toxic chemical (Cl- + 2OH– = ClO- + H2O + 2e-). The undesirable cation (such as Na+, Mg2+, and Ca2+) poison in seawater at cathode electrode (HER) is most critical challenge in stability and durability of seawater splitting. Hence, the corrosion and poisoning of electrocatalysts by chloride ions, as well as the formation of insoluble precipitates from various cations in seawater, significantly degrade the stability and durability of seawater electrolysis. To address these challenges and enhance the stability of electrocatalysts, the formation of a blocking or perm-selective layer represents a promising strategy to protect the electrocatalysts from unwanted reactions with various ions present in seawater. Reduced graphene hydrogel (GH) has been extensively studied as an excellent desalination membrane due to its high resistance barrier against seawater resources [21,22]. The highly porous structure of GH facilitates diffusion kinetics, enhancing the transport of intermediates and electrolytes toward the active sites and the release of product gases. Furthermore, the incorporation of phosphorus-doped graphene quantum dots (PGQDs) can effectively mitigate the corrosion of electrocatalysts in seawater electrolysis, as the P-C bond structure exhibits strong repulsive interactions with chloride ions. Therefore, the combination of PGQDs and GH composites can demonstrate synergistic performance for seawater electrolysis, where GH provides a 3D conductive framework for the uniform deposition of PGQDs.

In this study, we present the synthesis of P-doped graphene quantum dots/reduced graphene hydrogel (PGQDs/GH) composites and investigate their dual roles as photocatalysts and electrocatalysts for seawater splitting to produce hydrogen (H2). PGQDs were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal reaction, resulting in a high P-atomic content of up to 5 %. Varying hydrothermal temperatures led to different configuration of P-C bonds in the PGQDs. The influence of these different P-C bonds on the optical and electrical properties was studied, revealing that PGQDs with a predominantly PC3-bond configuration exhibited the smallest band gap. Subsequently, the PGQDs were incorporated into GH via a hydrothermal process to prepare the PGQDs/GH composites. The resulting composites exhibited a high surface area, highly porous structure, and numerous conductive pathways, thereby improving charge transfer and reaction kinetics. In terms of photocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), the PGQDs/GH composites demonstrated excellent performance in seawater electrolysis, achieving an H2 production yield of 20.47 mmol·g−1·h−1. The enhanced photocatalytic HER activities can be attributed to the effective separation of photo-induced holes and electrons between the PGQDs and GH, as confirmed by various characterization techniques including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, time-resolved fluorescence spectra, transient photocurrent, and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR).Moreover, the prepared PGQDs/GH composites exhibited remarkable bifunctional electrocatalytic activities for both HER and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). In alkaline media, the synthesized PGQDs/GH composites exhibited an overpotential of 178 mV at a high current density of 300 mA·cm−2 for HER and an overpotential of 289 mV at a current density of 20 mA·cm−2 for OER. These performances surpassed those of commercial Pt/C and IrO2 catalysts. Additionally, in a symmetric electrode system for seawater electrolysis, the PGQDs/GH electrodes demonstrated a low voltage of 1.62 V at 100 mA·cm−2, outperforming toward the commercial Pt/C//IrO2 electrodes (1.85 V). Impressively, after continuous seawater electrolysis operation for 1000 h at 500 mA·cm−2, the PGQDs/GH catalyst exhibited only approximately 5 % voltage degradation, indicating excellent stability and durability for seawater electrolysis. Density functional theory (DFT)computations revealed that the PC3-bond configuration in the PGQDs can tune the electronic structure which is an optimal structure for enhanced visible absorption, favored thermodynamic toward hydrogen and oxygen adsorption/desorption, and excellent stability, contributing to the improved photocatalytic and electrocatalytic activities.

All chemicals and solvents were used as received without any further purification. Deionized water (DI) produced by a Milli-Q Millipore system (18.2 MΩ, Millipore Corp, Billerica, MA) was used in all the experiments. Triphenylphosphine, glucose, Nafion (20 wt%), and chemical substances were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PGQDs and PGQD/GH composites
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 500 mg of glucose in 50 mL ethanol was mixed with 20 mg of triphenylphosphine in 40 mL ethanol. The mixture then was stirred for 30 min and transferred into 200 mL of steel autoclave high pressure reactor. Ar gas was purged for 30 min to remove all dissolved O2 in the mixture solution, the reactor was then heated at 180, 210, and 240 °C for 12 h. The synthesized PGQDs samples were denoted as PGQD1, PGQD2, and PGQD3 by different synthesis temperature, respectively. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained products were purified by keeping in a dialysis tube (3000 Da, Spectrum Lab. Inc) for 24 h. The final product was dispersed in ethanol by ultrasonication for 30 min. For a control sample, the pristine GQDs was prepared under the same procedure without the addition of triphenylphosphine. For the preparation of PGQD/GH composite, 5 mL of PGQD3 solution in DI water (2 mg/mL) was added slowly into 50 mL of graphene oxide (GO; GO-V30-100, Standard Graphene Inc.) suspension (4 mg/mL) with stirring and sonication for 1 h. The mixture suspension was transferred into 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained product was then treated by means of freeze-drying for 24 hr. The final product of PGQD/GH was obtained after further thermal annealing at 350 °C under N2 atmosphere.

Topography of PGQDs deposited on a mica substrate was studied using taping mode of atomic force microscopy (AFM; Veeco, Dimension 3100). The nanostructures were characterized with a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100F) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM). The element mapping was measured using the spherical aberration transmission electron microscope (ACTEM; PEI Titan Cubed Themis G2 300) which was equipped with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Thermo Fisher) measurements were performed using monochromatic CuKα radiation. UV–Vis absorption spectra were collected with Specord 210 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Raman spectra was acquired using DXR Raman spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) from 1000 to 2000 cm−1 at room temperature with a 532 nm excitation source. The resolved fluorescence lifetime experiment was undergone through a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) with a Spex Fluorolog-3 (Horiaba). Fluorescence spectroscopy was taken at room temperature using a Spex Fluorolog-3 Luminescence spectrophotometer at 365 nm excitation. The surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volume were measured in liquid nitrogen using Quadrasorbapparatus (Quantachrome Instruments) by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method (BET). The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were carried out using EPR spectrometer (JES-FA100, JEOL) under vacuum.

For a typical procedure, 20 mg of the synthesized catalysts was added into 100 mL of freshwater or seawater with 10 mL solution of 0.1 M Na2S and 0.1 M Na2SO3 as a sacrificial agent. The as-obtained suspension was sonicated for 15 min and then transferred to a Pyrex top-irradiation air-tight glass reactor which was sealed in a closed-gas circulation system. The reactor was purged five times by N2 and vacuum pumped to remove all dissolved gas in the suspension. The reactor cell was placed under 300 W Xenon lamp source with 400 nm cut-off filter (Sol1A, ORIEL). The suspension was stirred and irradiated at the power density of 100 mW·cm−2 and was maintained at 25 °C by circulating water bath, which is similar with AM 1.5 G illumination as one sun illumination. The produced gas was extracted at regular intervals of time by a gas-tight syringe and directly injected into a gas chromatography (6500 GC with TCD, YL Instrument, ultrapure N2 as carrier gas) for evaluating the produced amount of H2. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of PGQDs/GH composites for photocatalytic HER was determined by using a 450 nm ± 10 nm band-pass filter to convert the irradiation from simulated sunlight to monochromatic light. The AQE was estimated by following the equation
AQE
( % )
=
( n u m b e r o f g e n e r a t e d h y d r o g e n m o l e c u l e s x 2 )
( n u m b e r o f i n c i d e n t p h o t o s )
x 100 %

All electrochemical measurements were taken by a standard three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (Biologic VSP) at room temperature. Typically, the three-electrode cell includes an L-shape glass carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter), a pure graphite rod counter electrode, and a saturated calomel reference electrode (SCE). To prepare a working electrode, 5 mg of the obtained catalysts were mixed into the solution of 980 μL ethanol and 20 μL Nafion (5 % in isopropanol). The mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min to form homogeneous catalyst ink. The 5 μL of catalyst ink was casted on GCE and dried at room temperature. All electrochemical tests were performed in Ar-saturated 1 M KOH as the electrolyte at the catalyst loading mass of 0.3 mg·cm−2. The electrocatalytic activity for HER and OER was determined by employing the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) at a scan rate of 5 mV·s−1. All recorded potentials were converted to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) by using Nernst equation. The electrochemically active specific area (ECSA) of catalysts was determined through the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) by measuring the cyclic voltammetry (CV) at non-Faradaic potential with various scanning rates. The ECSA were calculated as follow:
ECSA =
C dl
/
C s
where Cs is defined as capacitance measured on a flat standard of 1 cm2 of real surface area and, the general Cs for carbon material is 0.0209 mF·cm−2.

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed at overpotential of 100 mV for HER and overpotential of 400 mV for OER with AC amplitude of 5 mV within the frequency from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. The long-term stability of the prepared catalysts was tested via chronopotentiometry at 15 mA·cm−2 for 110 h and 8000 CV cycles at scan rates of 50 mV·s−1. For overall seawater electrolysis, the alkaline electrolysis cell in mixed solution (1.0 M KOH in seawater) was assembled in two-electrodes air-tight H-type cell with a Nafion membrane. The typical electrode for seawater electrolysis was fabricated by spraying the PGDQ3/GH solution onto 2x2 cm2 Ni foam at 80 °C. The loading mass density of all samples was maintained at 0.3 mg·cm−2. The seawater was collected from local sea in Ulsan city, Korea. All results were calibrated by iR compensation following the equation.
E c
=
E m
-
i ∙ R
s
where Ec is the corrected potential, Em and i are actual potential and current obtained from LSV curve and Rs is the resistance from EIS measurements which were performed at −0.2 V vs. RHE with AC amplitude of 0.02 V and the frequency from 1 Hz to 100 kHz.

Density functional theory (DFT)-based calculations were studied using Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) Generalized Gradient approximation (GGA) [23] with Quantum Espresso software [24]. To describe the ionic cores, ultrasoft pseudopotentials were used for all the elements from PSLibrary [25] with energy cutoffs of 45 and 450 Ry for wavefunctions and charge density, respectively. Long range Van der Waals corrections were made through DFT-D3 approach [26]. the unit cell of GQDs was placed in a sufficiently large box of 30 Å where the GQDs were assumed to be isolated using Martyna-Tuckerman correction [27]. The size of the GQDs affects the reaction barriers, but it does not alter the trend of overpotential [28]. Therefore, the GQDs used here was of the smallest size, C24H12, with a zig-zag edge and 0.957 nm of diameter. Γ-point algorithm was used for Brillouin zone (BZ) sampling [28]. The total energies and forces for ionic minimization were converged within the threshold of 10-4 and 10-3 Ry, respectively, using BFGS quasi-newton algorithm, while the convergence criteria for self-consistency was set to 10-10 Ry for all calculations. The ordinary Gaussian spreading with a degauss value of 0.01 Ry was used for BZ integration. The climbing image-nudged elastic band (CI-NEB) calculations were carried out to determine the kinetic energy barriers for dissociation of water over PGQDs. The kinetic energy barriers for dissociation of water over PGQDs were determined using climbing image-nudged elastic band (CI-NEB) calculations [29] and XCrysDen software was chosen for the structure visualization [30].

Scheme1 illustrates the procedure of synthesis of PGQDs through hydrothermal method, where the glucose as a carbon source and triphenylphosphine as P-atom source are used to synthesize PGQDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was measured for the morphological study of the obtained products. As presented in Fig. 1 a and Fig. S1, the synthesized GQDs and PGQDs have similar spherical shape with narrow particle size distribution and average diameter of around 4.5 nm. Also, the height profiles of all samples in Fig. S2 have similar thickness of around 1.6 nm, indicating a few layers of graphene. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) of PGQD3 in Fig. 1b displays clear crystalline lattice fringes with an in-plane lattice parameter of approximately 0.24 nm, assigned to the (1120) crystal phase of graphite. Aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) was further employed to define the P-atoms on the carbon framework of GQDs. In the HAADF-STEM images with sub-angstrom resolution of PGQD3 (Fig. 1c), it is observed that the bigger and brighter dots marked by red circles are uniformly distributed on the graphene network, suggesting the successful formation P doped GQDs. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) elemental mapping images (Fig. 1d) confirmed that the P-atoms are uniformly placed on the lattice of C-atoms which is consistent with the above HRTEM image.

To examine the influence of the bond configurations of P-atoms, XPS spectra were collected. From the high resolution XPS elemental survey spectra (Fig. S3), P-atomic peaks are not observed for GQD, whereas PGQDs display P-atomic peaks with the contents of 4.19, 4.57 and 4.82 at% for PGQD1, PGQD2, and PGQD3, respectively (Table S1). It demonstrates successfully incorporating P-atoms into the carbon skeleton of graphene lattice and higher P-atomic contents as the increase of synthesis temperature. For further investigation, high resolution XPS C1s and P2p of different PGQDs samples were measured. As presented in Fig. 2 a-c, the high resolution C1s spectra of PGQDs could be deconvoluted into four peaks which centered at 284.4, 286.7, 287.4, and 288.9 eV were assigned to sp2-carbon aromatic C = C, C-O, C = O and O-C = O bonds, respectively. With the different synthesis temperature increasing from 180 to 240 °C, the peaks corresponding to C-P bond exhibit shift from 285.8 to 285.1 eV. This negative shift may be attributed to electron donation of P-atom to C-atom, which leads to larger electron cloud density on C-atoms. The high-resolution P2p XPS spectra of different PGQDs samples were analyzed to gain insights into the configuration of P-atomic bonds (Fig. 2d-f). For PGQD3, a single peak centered at 132.5 eV was observed, corresponding to a graphite-like PC3-bonding structure. In contrast, PGQD1 and PGQD2 samples exhibited different P-C bond configurations at 134.4 and 133.9 eV, respectively, which were assigned to phosphate (PO4) and phosphonate (PO3) bond structures. These observations indicate that PGQD3 synthesized at 240 °C primarily consists of PC3-structures, whereas PO4and PO3bond structures dominate in PGQD1 and PGQD2 synthesized at lower temperatures of 180 and 210 °C, respectively. The PGQD was further synthesized by different P-based susbtances to evaluate effect of dopants on P bonding structure. As depicted on Fig. S4 and Table S2, PGQDs prepared with triphenylphosphine contains mostly PC3-structure compared to other doping source, signifying key role of triphenylphosphine to achieve single type bonding of PC3 in PGQD. The observed negative shift in binding energy suggests a change in bonding structure, where the PC3 configuration allows for efficient electron transfer from P to C-atoms due to the lower electronegativity of P-atoms. In PGQD3 containing PC3-bonds dominantly, electron redistribution and polarization occur effectively, leading to improved electrical conductivity, electronic configuration, water absorption, and water dissociation, thereby enhancing the catalytic activity for water splitting.

The optical properties of PGQDs were studied via UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the strong absorption peak of pristine GQDs is observed at 360 nm which is red-shifted to 370 and 380 nm for PGQD1 and PGQD2, respectively. Moreover, PGQD3 exhibits more red-shifted absorption peak at 445 nm and a new absorption peak at 538 nm, indicating that PGQD3 show the alternation in electronic structure and the enhanced visible light absorption. By using Tauc plot for the determination of optical bandgap in Fig. 3b, the decrease of bandgap is observed for PGQDs synthesized at higher temperature and PGQD3 has the lowest band gap, compared to that of pristine GQD. These decrease in optical bandgap can be attributed to the electron donation from P-atoms into graphene lattice that can lead orbital rearrangement and generate bigger sp2 hydridization and new energy level contributed from sp3state of P-bonding configuration [31,32]. Additionally, the electrochemical-based band gap measurement in Fig. 3c reveals the similar bandgap change for PGQDs and the smallest band gap of 2.12 eV is observed for PGQD3, suggesting a promising potential of PGQDs in photocatalyst application using enhanced light absorption. Fig. 3d shows the normalized photoluminescence (PL) spectra of GQDs and PGQDs under the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The PL peak position of GQDs is observed at 460 nm, which is clearly red-shifted for PGQDs and lowest peak position for PGQD3. In addition, the synthesized PGQDs achieved higher quantum yields with the increase of P-atomic content, compared to GQDs (Table S3). These results are ascribed to modification of electron configuration by the electron donation from P-atoms and different P-atomic bond structures which can be controlled by synthesis temperature. The proposed synthesis method here can be an effective strategy for modulating optical band gap and electronic properties of GQDs.

The PGQDs/GH composites were prepared using a simple hydrothermal method using synthesized PGQDs and graphene oxides (Scheme 1). During the hydrothermal reaction, the PGQDs can be strongly attached on graphene oxide sheets of GH through π-π stacking interaction. The FESEM image of PGQD3/GH (Fig. 4 a) reveals a well-assembled and highly porous 3D nano-architecture with numerous submicrometer-sized holes. This unique structure of PGQDs/GH composites provides open channels for efficient transport of electrolytes, gases, or reactants. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to confirm the presence of PGQDs on the surface of GH. TEM images of the PGQD3/GH composite (Fig. 4b and c) demonstrate well-defined PGQDs uniformly attached and dispersed on graphene nanosheets, without agglomeration. This indicates successful decoration of PGQDs on the porous GH surface. Additionally, elemental dispersion X-ray (EDX) analysis (Fig. 4d) confirms the presence of C, O, and P atoms, further validating the presence of PGQDs. The highly porous structure of the PGQDs/GH composite was further investigated using N2 isothermal adsorption and desorption measurements (Fig. S5a). The isothermal curves of GH and PGQD3/GH composite exhibit clear hysteresis with a type IV isotherm shape, indicating the presence of primary mesopores and micropores within the hydrogels. Pore distribution and surface area were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) model (Fig. S5b). The surface area of GH and PGQD3/GH was determined to be 412 and 434 m2·g−1, respectively, with average pore sizes of 19 and 10 nm, respectively. These results suggest that the highly porous 3D structure of GH is maintained during the preparation process of PGQDs/GH composites. The observed characteristics indicate that the highly porous structure of the PGQDs/GH composite facilitates effective mass transport of electrolytes, gases, and intermediates, thereby enhancing catalytic performance.

Transient photocurrent measurements were performed to investigate the photoinduced charge separation efficiency of PGQDs/GH composites. Fig. 5 a shows the photocurrent responses curves from chronoamperometry analysis (i-t) with light on–off for GH and PGQDs/GH composites. PGQDs/GH composites exhibit higher photocurrent responses than GQDs/GH composite and the highest photocurrent is observed for PGQD3/GH composite, suggesting highest photoinduced charge separation efficiency. In addition, the PL emission and the time-resolved PL (TR-PL) spectra for PGQD3/GH composite were measured to explore the recombination process of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. Low PL emission intensity indicates efficient separation efficiency and low recombination of photoinduced charges. As shown in Fig. 5b, the PGQD3 exhibits high emission peak intensity at ca. 560 nm with the 365 nm-excitation. The similar PL spectrum is observed for PGQD3/GH composite, but its emission intensity is significantly reduced, suggesting that the PGQD3/GH composite demonstrates the effective separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The obtained TR-PL spectra for PGQD3 and PGQD3/GH composite are fitted by a tri-exponential decay function (Fig. 5c). The average decay lifetimes of PGQD3 and PGQD3/GH composite is determined to be 5.95 and 9.19 ns, respectively (inset). This longer fluorescence lifetime indicates the effective charge separation and the reduction of electron-hole recombination of PGQD3/GH composite, which can promote the enhanced photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic H2 production measurements were first carried out with different sacrificial agents in seawater using PGQD3/GH composite (200 mg/L). The negligible H2 production is observed without sacrificial agent and the maximum H2 production is shown for the mixture of 0.1 M of Na2S and 0.1 M of Na2SO3 (Fig. S6a). Also, the noticeable difference of H2 production is not observed for fresh water splitting and seawater splitting, suggesting the sacrificial agents have no additional reaction toward seawater (Fig. S6b). The photocatalytic activity of seawater slitting for the prepared photocatalysts is further measured with the sacrificial agents of 0.1 M Na2S and 0.1 M Na2SO3. The photocatalytic activity for the H2 production of seawater electrolysis was examined for the prepared photocatalysts of PGQDs/GH composites under visible light irradiation. The time-dependent H2 production and the corresponding H2 production rate with apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for the prepared composite samples are presented in Fig. 6 a and Fig. S7, respectively. No noticeable H2 production is observed for GH and GQDs/GH samples, and a slight increase is for PGQD1/GH and PGQD2/GH composite, indicating low photocatalytic activity for H2 production. However, it is surprising that the PGQD3/GH composite demonstrates a significantly increased H2 production rate of 20.47 mmol·h−1·g−1 with highest AQE of 35 %, suggesting excellent photocatalytic H2 production activities of PGQD3/GH composite from seawater electrolysis. It is obviously shown that the PC3-bonds of PGQD3 is more favorable for the H2 production reaction than other PO4 and PO3 bonds of PGQD1 and PGQD2, respectively. These results are ascribed to the efficient photoinduced charge separation of PGQD3/GH composite and small bandgap of PGQD3 for enhanced visible light absorption. Fig. 6b shows the H2 production rate with different concentration of PGQD3/GH composite ranging from 80 to 240 mg/L. The H2 production rate is increased with the loading concentration and the 200 mg/L loading of PGQD3/GH composite exhibits the highest H2 production rate. The decrease of H2 production rate is shown for the higher loading, which is due to the reduced light absorption of turbid seawater by excess photocatalyst particles. The H2 production capability of PGQD3/GH composite is compared with previously reported other metal-based and carbon-based photocatalysts (Table S4), which shows the superior photocatalytic activity for H2 production of PGQD3/GH composite, compared to the reported photocatalyst. Besides, the five cycles were conducted to verify the stability of PGQD3/GH composite in seawater electrolysis. The consequent 3-cycles for 6 h and 4th-5th cycles were then tested carried at 7-days interval. As shown in Fig. 6c, PGQD3/GH composite exhibits excellent photocatalytic stability with maintaining high H2 production rate. To confirm the reactive radicals in the photocatalytic process of H2 production of PGQD3/GH composite, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy was conducted with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) used as a trapping agent for ∙O2 – radicals. Results depicted in Fig. 6d show that distinct ESR signal peaks of DMPO-∙O2 -products are observed under visible light irradiation, while no signal peak is observed in the dark, indicating that the ∙O2 – radicals are the dominant reactive radicals in the photocatalytic H2 production using PGQD3/GH composite. Fig. 6e illustrates a possible photocatalytic mechanism for PGQD3/GH composite. The heterojunction structure of PGQD3/GH composite facilitates the rapid transfer and separation of photoexcited electrons-holes, which results in efficient photocatalytic activity. Specifically, photoexcited electrons of PGQDs are transferred from the conduction band (CB) of PGQDs to the CB of graphene where the electrons react with H2O and O2 molecules resulting in the production of H2 and ∙O2 – radicals. Simultaneously, the sacrificial agent of Na2S/Na2SO3 can be oxidized by the photoexcited holes to form SO4 2- ions, which reduces the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and promotes the efficiency of H2 production reaction. The enhanced photocatalytic capacity of PGQD3/GH composite is attributed to the controlled bandgap of PGQDs and π-π interaction between PGQDs and GH, which allows for efficient transfer and separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs.

Beside the photocatalytic performance, the electrochemical hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activity of PGQD/GH composite was further examined in 1.0 M KOH using a three-electrode configuration. For comparison, the commercial Pt/C catalyst (20 wt%) was also evaluated under the same experimental condition. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves for the prepared catalysts in Fig. 7 a obviously shows that PGQD3/GH composite exhibits lower overpotential of 41 mV than other composite samples and comparable to the commercial Pt/C catalyst (36 mV) at a current density of 10 mA∙cm−2. In comparing to the reported transition and noble metal-based catalysts, PGQD3/GH composite demonstrates lower overpotential (Table S5). The Tafel analysis is employed to understand the HER reaction kinetics. The corresponding Tafel slopes are presented in Fig. 7b.The Tafel slope of PGQD3/GH composite is calculated to be 39 mV∙dec-1 which is smaller than that of other prepared catalyst and comparable value to Pt/C (35 mV∙dec-1), which indicates fast HER reaction kinetic of PGQD3/GH composite following Volmer-Heyrocsky as rate determining step. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were further performed to study the charge transfer kinetics for HER. The Nyquist plots of prepared catalysts in Fig. 7c show that the PGQD3/GH composite the smallest diameter of semicircle among the composite samples. The fitted EIS results for the series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) are summarized in Table S6, where the PGQD3/GH composite exhibits smaller Rct value of 48 Ω than those of other samples. These results indicate superior HER activity of the PGQD3/GH composite with low charge transfer resistance as well as fast charge transfer for HER, which is consistence with the LSV results and Tafel plots. Moreover, it can be observed that the PC3-bonds of PGQD3possess outstanding electrocatalytic activity as well as photocatalytic activity for H2 production, compared PO3 and PO4 bonds of other PGQDs. The electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) is one of important parameters to evaluate the HER electrocatalytic activity. The ECSA of prepared catalyst are calculated from the double layer capacitance value (Cdl) using CV curves measured in the non-Faradic region at various scanning rates ranging from 40 to 80 mV∙s−1 (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. 7d, the PGQD3/GH composite exhibits the highest Cdl (39 mF∙cm−2) and ECSA values (1866 cm−2) among the prepared catalysts (Table S7), suggesting the abundant active sites and a large specific surface area resulting in superior HER activity. In addition, the ECSA normalized HER activity, turnover frequency (TOF) and mass activity of different PGQD/GH catalysts (Fig. S9 and Table S6) were estimated. After normalization, PGQD3/GH still exhibits the lowest overpotential for electrochemically HER catalytic activity. The TOF and mass activity of PGQD3/GH were calculated to be 13.89 s−1 at 200 mV and 256.63 A·mg−1 at 50 mV, respectively, which is much higher than those of other catalysts, indicating excellent intrinsic catalytic performance. The stability and durability of PGQD3/GH catalyst were evaluated using the chronopotentiometry test at a current density of 15 mA∙cm−2 for 110 h (Fig. 7e). The results show the minimal and negligible change of overpotential is observe for PGQD3/GH catalyst under continuously working condition, while the noticeable potential degradation is recorded for the commercial Pt/C catalyst. Moreover, as presented in Fig. 7f, the LSV curves before and after 8000 CV cycles display minor decay of current density, which further demonstrates the superior durability and stability of PGQD3/GH composite for promising HER catalysts.

To explore the bifunctional catalytic activity of PGQD/GH composites, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) catalytic activities of the prepared catalysts were also studied using a typical three-electrode system in a 1.0 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. For comparison, the commercial IrO2 catalyst was also evaluated under the same experimental condition. From the LSV curves in Fig. 8 a, the PGQD3/GH catalyst exhibits lower overpotential of 262 mV at a current density of 10 mA∙cm−2 than other prepared catalysts and the commercial IrO2 (300 mV). The corresponding Tafel plots in Fig. 8b display that the PGQD3/GH catalyst also show lower Tafel slope value (64 mV∙dec-1), compared to other catalysts and IrO2 catalyst (77 mV∙dec-1). These excellent OER activities of PGQD3/GH catalyst outperform to the previously reported carbon-based catalysts (Table S8). These results are attributed to higher number of active sites from PC3-bonds of PGQD3/GH catalyst with large specific surface area, resulting in a favorable activity and faster reaction kinetics for OER as well as HER. From EIS measurement (Fig. 8c), the Rct of the PGQD3/GH was calculated to be 64 Ω which is smaller than those of other samples (Table S9), suggesting fast electron transfer and mass transfer of PGQD3/GH during the OER process. In addition, we evaluated the Cdl values from CV measurement at different scan rats (Fig. S10). the Cdl and ESCA of PGQD3/GH composite are calculated to be the highest value of 22 mF·cm−2 and 1052 cm−2 among all prepared catalysts, respectively, contributing the abundance of accessed active sites (Fig. 8d and Table S10). After normalizing to ECSA (Fig. S11), the OER activity of PGQD3/GH remains the best catalytic performance compared with other catalyst, signifying the superior intrinsic activity toward OER. The turnover frequency (TOF) and mass activity have been considered as important factor for accessing efficiency. TOF and mass activity of PGQD3/GH was obtained to be 2.051 s−1 and 42.23 A·g−1 at 1.5 V, respectively, significantly outperforming other catalysts, confirming exceptional electrochemically catalytic activity of the composite. For the stability of PGQD3/GH catalyst, the chronoaperometric response of the sample was measured at a current density of 15 mA∙cm−2 for 110 h (Fig. 8e). The results show no significant degradation of potential for 110 h, which the IrO2 catalyst an obvious decay of potential, indicating the effective long-term stability of PGQD3/GH catalyst. Moreover, the CV curve change of PGQD3/GH catalyst was monitored after 8000 CV cycles, and only a minimal change of CV curve is observed (Fig. 8f), showing an excellent durability of PGQD3/GH catalyst for OER. Moreover, in order to assess the OER activity and selectivity of the prepared PGQD3/GH in real seawater condition, the electrochemical performance was carried out in different electrolytes, such as 1.0 M KOH, 1.0 M KOH + 3.0 M NaCl, and 1.0 M KOH + seawater electrolytes. As depicted in Fig. 8g, the polarization curves of PGQD3/GH represent the overpotentials of 370 and 380 mV at current density of 500 mA·cm−2 in 3.0 M NaCl and seawater, respectively, and the insignificant decay of overpotential (368 mV) is observed in pure alkaline electrolyte, indicating excellent selectivity and high resistance of PGQD3/GH toward seawater poisoning. The chronopotentiometry measurements of PGQD3/GH in various electrolytes were performed at the current density of 100 mA·cm−2 to explore the long-term stability under chloride poisoning and seawater pollutant conditions. As shown in Fig. 8h, the PGQD3/GH exhibit excellent potential stability operation in alkaline (96 %), 3.0 M NaCl (94 %), and seawater (92 %) electrolytes during 100 h without any significant potential decay. Moreover, the LSV measurements of OER were carried out in various range pH condition of seawater to uncover the electrocatalytic activity of PGQD3/GH (Fig. 8i and Fig. S12). The PGQD3/GH catalyst delivers similar overpotentials of 379, 383, and 385 mV to obtain the current density of 500 mA·cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH (pH = 13), 0.1 M H2SO4 (pH = 2), and PBS (pH = 7.5), respectively, indicating the potential application of PGQD3/GH catalyst in wide pH range. These results demonstrate the excellent electrocalytic activity, remarkable stability and robust corrosion resistance of PGQD3/GH catalyst for seawater electrolysis.

Encouraged by outstanding HER and OER eletrocatalytic performance, the two-electrode water electrolysis cell was evaluated using the PGQD3/GH catalyst as both cathode and anode prepared by spray coating on carbon clothes to investigate their water splitting performance of fresh water and seawater (Fig. 9 a). The commercial Pt/C catalyst as the cathode and IrO2as the anode was tested for comparison. Fig. 9b shows the CV curves of PGQD3/GH and Pt/C//IrO2catalysts in both 1.0 M KOH of fresh water solution and 1.0 M KOH of seawater solution. It can be obviously seen that the PGQD3/GH catalyst demonstrate almost the same overall water splitting activities in both fresh water and seawater, delivering the voltage of 1.43 V for fresh water and 1.47 V for sea water at current density of 10 mA·cm−2. In contrast, the Pt/C//IrO catalysts show inferior water splitting activities for fresh water (1.49 V) and seawater (1.57 V), which underperforms for both fresh water and seawater, compared to PGQD3/GH catalyst. Moreover, at a high current density of 500 mA·cm−2, the PGQD3/GH catalyst clearly outperforms the Pt/C//IrO2catalyst in both fresh water and sea water electrolytes. the PGQD3/GH catalyst delivers an applied voltage of 1.73 V for fresh water and 1.75 V for seawater to yield a high current density of 500 mA·cm−2, whereas the Pt/C//IrO2 catalyst shows a voltage of 1.98 and 2.20 V (Fig. S13), demonstrating its superior overall water splitting performances for fresh water and seawater. The voltage response of PGQD3/GH catalyst was measured in seawater electrolysis when the current density was elevated from 50 to 600 mA·cm−2. As show in Fig. 9c, the potentials rapidly levels off and stabilized with the increase of current density, indicating excellent mass transport and goodconductivityof PGQD3/GH catalyst. The Faradaic efficiency of the electrolyzer using PGQD3/GH composite in 1.0 M KOH seawater was evaluated by collecting the gas products evolved from the cathode and anode. As shown in Fig. S14, the molar production rate of H2 and O2 gases is close to 2:1 and its Faradaic efficiency is determined to be over 95 % for the seawater electrolysis. In addition, the only small amount of ClO- production is observed during the seawater electrolysis, suggesting that the Cl- evolution reaction is rarely occurred with PGQD3/GH electrodes (Fig. S15). In the comparison with previously reported catalysts, the PGQD3/GH composite exhibits superior water splitting activities to transitional and noble metals based catalysts, as summarized in Table S11. Moreover, the long-term stability of PGQD3/GH catalyst was further studied by the chronopotentiometric and chronoamperometric tests for 1000 h of continuous operation in a 1.0 KOH seawater solution at a high current density of 500 mA·cm-2and applied voltage of 1.62 V, respectively. As shown Fig. 9d and Fig. S16, thePGQD3/GH catalyst shows slight change of potential and current for 1000 h of seawater electrolysis, while the Pt/C//IrO2 catalysts exhibits significant degradation. The superior stability of PGQD3/GH composites for seawater electrolysis was further confirmed by HRTEM, Raman and XPS studies morphology and electronic structure after the cycle test. Interestingly, the structure of PGQD at anode and cathode maintain without any changes in lattice distance after long-term stability test (Fig. S17 and Fig. S18). When compared the fresh state composite, the high resolution XPS P2p spectra of PGQD3/GH electrode exhibit the same peaks without any peak shift (Fig. S19) and the Raman spectra peaks are also unaltered after the 1000 h cycle test (Fig. S20), implying the chemical and structural stability of PGQD3/GH composite in seawater splitting reaction. The outstanding stability of PGQD3/GH composite may be originated from hybridized p-p orbital in PC3 bonding, which accelerate electron transfer and charge redistribution. The P-atoms not only respond to regulate electronic structure by electron-donating behavior but also effectively manipulate chemical surface for the improved adsorption of OH–, which improves catalytic activities and reduce oxygen functionalities absorption on carbon to stabilize carbon structure. A practical approach was implemented employing a standard solar cell to directly convert the renewable energy source into sustainable H2 production. To evaluate the seawater electrolysis efficiency, three in-series Si solar cell panels (with an open circuit voltage of approximately 1.93 V in Fig. S21) were utilized to power the seawater electrolyzer using PGQD3/GH composites as both electrodes, as depicted in Fig. 9e. Under the sunlight exposure, the PGQD3/GH electrodes demonstrated consistent production of H2 and O2 (Fig. 9e and Video S1), indicating the conversion of solar energy into the practical H2 production from the seawater electrolysis. These evidences indicate that the PGQD3/GH composites can be a promising electrochemical catalyst for practical seawater electrolysis to produce the efficient H2 production.

To understand further the superior HER and OER activities of PGQD3/GH catalyst, the density functional theory (DFT) calculation was performed. The structural model of pristine and PGQDs with different bond structures are presented in Fig. S22. Based on above as-obtained HRTEM images and high-resolution XPS analysis, PGQD1 and PGQD2 models were constructed as GQD having a phosphate and phosphonate functional groups at edge, respectively, while PGQD3 contains one PC3 structure. To find the most favorable adsorption site for the free molecules on the GQD, we have considered all possible sites, including bridge, hollow, and on-top sites. The most stable configurations of reaction intermediates used to calculate the Gibbs free energy for OER and HER are shown in Fig. S23-S25. Fig. 10 a displays the Gibbs free energy profile of the synthesized PGQDs with different bond structures for OER process. In all cases, potential determining step (PDS) of OER was related to the generation of HOO* intermediates. Although PGQD2 exhibits the lower thermodynamic overpotential (ηTD ) of 1.33 V than PGQD3 (1.73 V), the calculated adsorption energy of –OOH on PGQD3 was positive (1.89 eV), which is significantly smaller than the PGQD2 (4.37 eV) (Table S12), indicating that the unfavorable adsorption of –OOH on PGQD2 may block the O2 evolution [33,34]. The more favorable adsorption of –OOH on PGQD3 than on PGQD2 was due to the undercoordinated environment adjacent to a phosphorus site of PGQD3, which was not found in PGQD2. Considering these results for PGQD2 and PGQD3, only PGQD3 was found to be a suitable candidate for the OER performance in the present study. In case of PGQD1, since it is noticed that –OOH adsorption resulted in H2O formation instead of O2 evolution, the free energy diagram for PGQD1 was not included in Fig. 10a. The stable configurations of different reaction intermediates on PGQD2 and PGQD3 were used to calculate the free energies for OER (Fig. S26). The further investigation was carried out to study the excellent OER performance of PGQD3. As shown in Fig. 10a, the adsorption of HO– on PGQD3 is the exothermic process and the other steps are exothermic. A similar trend for ΔG4 which is ΔG1 step in OER, was observed in graphene for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [35]. To offer insights into exothermic conditions for the rest of the steps, we considered two values of the applied bias, ηOER = 0 and 1.73 V. For ηOER = 0 V, the steps of M−OOH and M−O2are still uphill and thus a further increase of the applied bias is needed. It is noted that all steps become downhill for ηOER = 1.73 V. Therefore, the ηTD of 1.73 V is required to make all exothermic steps for PGQD3 (Fig. S26). For H (O, OH, and OOH) adsorption on PGQD3, the C (P) site was most preferable. It should be noted that the ηTD predicts the activity trends observed in the experiments. To support the preferred OER mechanism over PGQD3, the CI-NEB calculations was performed to check the energy required for dissociation of water molecule over PGQD3 (Fig. S27). The reaction kinetic energy barrier of 2.13 eV is found to be lower than the H2O dissociation over graphene surface which was reported between 3.53 and 4.4 eV [36–38], indicating the superior OER activity of PGQD3. These DFT study supports our experimental results that the PGQD3 favors both dissociative chemisorption and physisorption of a water molecule for OER performance. among the PGQDs in this study was found for PGQD3 of 0.22 eV. The in situ electrochemical assisted Raman spectroscopy was measured in 1.0 M KOH at 1.75 V using two-electrode electrolyzer to evaluate chemical structure during reaction (Fig. S28). Two typical peaks were observed at 638 and 750 cm−1, corresponding to P-C and P-O vibration model, respectively. For the initial states of PGQD3/GH catalyst before reaction, the P-C vibration peak is only observed owing to the existence of double layer on the electrode surface, causing the weak intermediate adsorption. After 2 and 4 s of cell operation, the P-O vibration peak appear immediately in both HER and OER electrodes, indicating a strong bonding of OH* intermediate species on P atoms during electrochemical reaction. The DFT-predicted HER performance of PGQD3 showed good agreement with our experimental findings. As presented in Fig. S29, the charge density different and electrostatic potential map (EPM) of PGQD3 model reveal electron delocalization and charge redistribution of P-C bonding. Compared with other neighbor C, the C atoms of PC3 structure possess increased negative charge, due to electron transfer between C and P induced by p-p orbital coupling. For comprehensive understanding electronic-state-related electrocatalytic mechanism, the density of state (DOS) and projected partial density of state (PDOS) calculation were performed to investigate the effect of different P bonding structure on the electronic modulation of GQD. It is clearly shown in Fig. 10c that the states around Fermi level increase significantly in the presence of PC3-structure of PGQD3. The more states near Fermi level cannot only boost the conductivity but also enhance the charge transfer between electrode and intermediates, thus improving electrochemically catalytic activity. In contrast, GQD, PGQD1, and PGQD2 show lack of states near Fermi level, resulting in the poor catalytic activity. In particular, the PDOS study of P, C1 (atomic neighboring P) and C2 sites represent that only P-3p and C1-2p orbitals possess electronic distribution mainly around Fermi level (Fig. 10d). Due to the p-p orbital coupling effect, P-3p orbital modulates C1-2p orbital to broaden more states in wide range from valence to conducting states, essentially promoting the electron transfer and adjusting the hydrogen adsorption/desorption pathway, which is consistent with the Gibb free energy calculation. In addition, the P-3p orbital shows the most upshift of P-band center Ɛp toward Fermi level. It suggests that the introduction PC3-bond in GQD can promote robust valence states and reduce workfunction, resulting to smaller band gap, the enhanced water adsorption, and OER electrochemical activity. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) and XPS valence spectroscopy (Fig. S30) were employed to evaluate workfunction and valence energy level of GQDs and PGQD3. The calculated workfunctions and valence band values of PGQD3 were determined to be 4.18 and 1.56 eV, respectively, which is lower than those of GQDs (4.64 and 1.98 eV), facilitating the electron exchange between catalysts and intermediates as well as narrowing the band gap. Those results are consistent with above PDOS and optical properties. Furthermore, the Ab-inito molecular dynamics was employed to verify thermodynamic stability of PGQD3 under the harsh working condition (Fig. S31). At temperature of 500 K, the total energy of system and the P-C bonding length just show small amplitude fluctuations around −204 eV and 1.74 \AA, respectively, demonstrating highly stable and robust chemical structure of PGQD3. Furthermore, potentiodynamic polarization test of PGQD3/GH in 3.0 M NaCl was conducted to investigate saline resistance. As presented in Fig. 10e, PGQD3/GH coated steel shows the decrease in corrosion current and positively shifting in corrosion potential compared with pure steel, demonstrating effective barriers against chloride ion intrusion. In addition, the DFT computation was performed for the adsorption energy of Cl- ion on different PGQDs structures including PGQD1, PGQD2, and PGDQ3, as shown in Fig. S32. As expected, the PGQD3 structure reveals a positive value, indicating high repellent for chloride ions, while PGQD1 and PGQD2 exhibit negative absorption energies. These indicates that the PC3-sites play role as protecting protecting-catalyst-surface sites from corrosion, which is consistence with reported studies [40–42]. Combining these results, the water-splitting mechanism model for alkaline seawater involves two primary steps as depicted in Fig. 10f. First, the water molecules are favorably adsorbed on P atoms of PC3-structures and dissociated into OH* on P sites and H* on neighbor C atoms, while PC3-bonds exhibit high adsorption resistance to chloride and metal ions. Finally, H* is converted to hydrogen molecules by the Volmer-Heyrovsky model, and OH* is electrochemically transformed into oxygen molecules by four-electron pathway.

In summary, our work has successfully synthesized PGQDs containing different P-configurations of PO4, PO3, and PC3-bonds and by varying synthesis temperature. Specifically, the PGQD3 containing PC3-bonds shows most tunable electronic structure which significantly enhances the visible light absorption. Thus, when combined with GH as highly conductive pathway, the composite performs an outstanding photocalytic activity toward H2 production in fresh water and seawater due to efficiency photo-generated charge interface transfer between PGQD3 and GH via π-π interaction. With excellent photocatalytic activities, PGQD3/GH composite performs excellent electrochemical HER and OER activities with small overpotential at 10 mA·cm−2 (41 mV for HER and 262 mV for OER) and excellent stability in 110 h operation with small degradation, compared with commercial catalysts. The constructed electrolysis cell possesses superior both water splitting and seawater splitting. In particular, the cell can be operated continuously in 1000 h at high current density of 500 mA·cm−2 in seawater with only marginal decay. It demonstrated that the PC3-bond structure plays key role of tunable electronic structure and modulated active sites. Combined with theoretical computation, it was found that electron donation from P atom to C atom lead to charge redistribution and electronic delocalization that promote the absorption of water and decrease the barrier energy for dissociation of water. Importantly, PC3-bond structures also contribute as corrosion barrier which prevents the electrode from Cl- corrosion. These results indicate that the prepared PGQD3/GH composites hold promise as corrosion-resistant materials in harsh environments, particularly in marine applications. The prepared PGQD3/GH composites in this work provides an insight into photocatalytic and electrocatalytic seawater electrolysis that may be fundamental study for design and synthesis non-metal catalyst for seawater electrolysis.

Jin Suk Chung: Resources. Seung Hyun Hur: Resources. Won Mook Choi: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
